title: Emily Gutzmer -Operations Interview - Bridget | Client Operations Manager - Agency
meeting_title: Emily Gutzmer -Operations Interview - Bridget | Client Operations Manager - Agency

transcript.text: I'm so sorry.
I was like deep in a slack with Andy, like back and forth, rapid fire.
And then all of a sudden I was, I just had this feeling like something was wrong.
Oh no.
Anyway, sorry, I'm late and I do have a hard stop.
So we'll try to get through everything quickly.
But I did read all of Andy's like notes and everything.
So I feel pretty caught up and I had looked at your background, which is awesome.
Um, I'm so excited to talk to you.
Same.
Yeah.
Cool.
Um, so I run ops here by the way, and, um, I work closely with Andy.
Um, and I previously ran like biz ops and rep ops at my last company.
So excited to help hire this role.
Cause I'm like big believer in having Swiss army knife people everywhere.
I'm really happy.
We're finally going for it.
Totally.
I bet that's exciting.
Yeah.
Um, so.
Anyways, excited to talk to you.
I really only have like two questions we'll go through, but we'll try to go deep on them.
And I did read some of your notes from Andy.
So like to the extent you have different examples from what you talked about with her, that would be also awesome.
So like the first thing I wanted to ask you was just, this is kind of a philosophical question, but when and at what point do you decide to automate something?
Like it's, you know, there's, it can be a polarizing topic of like going too far one way or not.
Like, do you have any mental models or like philosophies around that?
Yeah.
I mean, that's a good question.
Just like hopping right in.
The things that I think are low hanging fruit for automating are the things that just like take up time and don't necessarily add value for the people.
People who are doing the work.
And so that might be, you know, a deal closes for a project, for like a build, for example.
And at Airtable, we often built for clients.
They're like first workflow up and running in the platform.
So a deal for a build might close.
There's no reason to me that a project for that build can't automatically open.
Like no one needs to be going into the system and going, oh, here's a new closed deal.
Let me like manually go create the project.
Or even, you know, in this age with like AI driving so much, like I think Firefly's notes are such a great thing to just, you know, I write notes down anyway.

But it's always good to be able to go back and like what did they capture that I missed or like can I read the context around that one word that I wrote down and starred and just like complementing some of the work that I'm already doing anyway.

And so I really think of it as like that, like what brings people out of the admin work, out of the like, let me double check that I've crossed my T's and dotted my I's about getting those analogies mixed up.

But, you know, taking, taking people out of that so that they can actually like work with what they have in front of them as a human error to like, yeah, we have, I feel like we have very little automation right now.
Okay.
Part of it is because, and this goes, this is sort of philosophy, philosophy question, but like, like, is the right time to do it?
It's like, it's good.
I like having people feel like manual pain first to make sure that it's like, the process is working as it should.
And like, you trust, you trust that it's the right thing before you go and build it, I guess.
And, and also just forces people to be like very conscious of like what they're doing at every step of the way.
Yeah.
Also, automating a bit by bit is sometimes helpful for, like, from a change management perspective.
think people can get very set in.
This is how I do this work.
And you come in and automate everything.
And then suddenly people are like, I don't know where to find this.
Or I don't know what I'm supposed to do now.
Did this thing already get done?
So like kind of backing up and going, you know, if I automate one thing, does that take care of this pain point for you and let you skip ahead?
And just kind of chipping away at it, I think, can be helpful from that perspective as well.
So some of the examples you're talking to Andy about were like very big projects, which is awesome.
Do you have examples of like little automations you've built or like little things that you would prove?
Like I don't need it to be like a game changer to hold anything, but like just little things that you think of like day to day that are like have just made your people more efficient?
Yeah.
I think one thing that in the example that I talked to Andy about in ramping up the Zendesk platform, one of the, one of the pieces of it that we came up against is that we were opening up our.
So that account did not just have one point person, but they also had someone on the merchandising team who handled the tickets.
And they were also What's a merchandising team?
Yeah.
So what Curated for You did was like cross, for e-commerce platforms, cross category merchandising to hit like lifestyle points.

So they were the team going like, if we're trying to put a product, a big product page up for shopping for your vacation to Italy, they were helping to like pick out the dresses and the shoes and whatever needed to go on there.
So they were very like services oriented in that respect, but we were opening them up to be client facing, at least one person per account.
And they just had no idea how to talk to clients.
Like they were, a lot of them were fresh out of college.
This was one of their first or second jobs.

And so there was a whole skill set around, how are you client facing and diplomatic and not throwing the customer, like throwing us under the bus when we miss something, you know, just like, how do you speak through problems?
And, uh, so Ness has the opportunity to just like automate.
Like little snippets of messages.

So rather than teaching them, I mean, we talked a lot about like, you don't say I, you say we, because we as a company or, you know, like some of the stuff, but also just being able to offer them like, hey, just go click in and like add this snippet, then this snippet, then this snippet, and then make it sound like you said it a little bit, like smooth it out.
I think it helped a lot for getting them comfortable with seeing the verbiage that you would give to a customer.
And then they got more comfortable writing it themselves.
But it was just like, I mean, some of them were two sentences long.
Some of them were like, just put in the, so sorry about that pain point.
Like, let me take a look at this for you kind of language.
And then they didn't have to worry about drafting it until they were comfortable with that vocabulary.
Oh, that's good.

We have, we're in a really similar situation where we have a lot of, which we will change, but we don't have actually that many like commercially minded people like who have experience when relationships in the company, because we went really heavy on like.
Sorry, I'm sick.
Sorry, Sorry,

And on like editors, content writers, and then we're kind of just like through them at our customers, which is like now we're feeling the pain of that because we're coming up on renewal cycle, the first one ever.
And it's like, oh, you don't ask the customer if they're happy ever or like you don't know, you don't think triggers when like the stakeholder doesn't show up for three weeks.
really, I think like stuff I just take for granted because I was in like account management roles in the past.
Yeah.
So a lot of, and this is probably one where you wouldn't automate it at first.

It's more about like training and onboarding and stuff, but like how to get people more, like what are like little things we can do to make people more customer savvy until we either hire more people who are or like train people to catch up or whatever.
Yeah, or even being able to like train people on a manual input, like if your stakeholder doesn't show up, that might be it.
Checkmark to put somewhere.
But then what does that automate down the line?
Like, does that at some point in the future trigger a playbook?
Like, oh, that feels risky.
Let's like, we're looking at for this, this, this next call.
And just kind of like feeding it to people.
Exactly.
Exactly.
What, like newer tools, I don't know if newer is the right word, but like what kind of tools have you been experimenting with?
Like, not necessarily that like the whole org had to adopt it, like a Zendesk, that's like a huge purchasing decision.
But like, I don't know, you and your day to day, have you tried new tools or like anything that's, I don't know, you're finding exciting to use?
Yeah, I, uh, I'm trying to get out of my Airtable bubble.
I feel like I worked there for so long and I built so much in Airtable, but it's my default for everything.
Same with our CEO.
He's obsessed.
Really?
Um, yeah, I run, I run a lot out of it and it works so well for a lot of things.
And sometimes it's just so heavy for something that doesn't need to be like that.

I have been playing around with something called Motion, it might be Motion AI, but it is, I think it can do a lot more than what I'm using it for right now, but I'm using it for managing, it's like, it auto schedules tasks.

There's like an AI component where I can input, it hooks up to my calendar, I can input, here's a bunch of tasks I have to do, and some of them are big, like prep for an interview, and some of them are like, oh, I need to remember to take the trash out today kind of tasks.
But if I put in the priority, and the deadlines, and I let AI schedule it, it'll kind of like give me a day, like an agenda for the day.
And I don't know how much I like it, because I'm ultimately a to-do list person, like I want to write down what I have to do, and then scratch it off.
I don't, I am testing out like, does it feel like a good solution for me when I have to brain dump a bunch of stuff, and just like, get it in my agenda somewhere, if that kind of solves that problem.
But, um, I'm experimenting with that, and I'm experiencing mean- I
Experimenting with Obsidian as like a documentation tool, as a note taker.
Oh, nice.
Yeah.
Do you use Zapier at all?
I have.
I have played with it a little bit, honestly, in trying to connect Airtable to Motion, which was stupid.
I didn't need to do that.

But I've used it a little bit in trying to like connect a couple platforms, both in like my personal stuff, and then also it curated for you, trying to like trigger, if something closed in HubSpot, did that open an item in Notion for us, for example, and kind of connect those two tools.
Does that work well?
It was a little, at the time, it was a little clunkier than I wanted.
I think some of it was we didn't have super clean data inputs in HubSpot.
So I was sort of like having to invent the data inputs that would trigger the automations.
And so there was a little bit of like thought experiment and how effective it would be if people...
People weren't actually doing the original thing that they needed to do.
But once it got going, it worked pretty well.
We ended up not paying for Zapier.
So the limit for us from that end was just scale.
What else did they use instead?
Just Airtable or was this a different company?
The last company I was at was Curated for You.
was a different company.
So at Airtable, we used Airtable for so much.
Curated for You, we were using HubSpot and we were using Notion.
And we were using Linear for a lot of our internal stuff.
then Zendesk is what I added into the tech stack for customer-facing tasks to complete.
Did you have the whole team in HubSpot?
No.
Okay.
We were pretty limited on seats.
And that made it...
I don't know about you.
Coming out of customer success management side, we were all in Salesforce all the time.
We were doing a lot of updating in Salesforce.
And it was a robust, sometimes messy...
source of truth, but, like, it was a source of truth.
And because we only had, I think we only had, like, 10 seats in HubSpot.
HubSpot just didn't serve that purpose for us yet.
And so we were kind of limiting.
track, like, how did you, like, well, maybe this was in your notes with Andy, but what was, like, where would the team put, like, if a customer was at risk?
Like, where would that live?
For a long time, it was in their personal notes, and it drove me up the wall.
One of the things that, when I ramped up the customer health score, one of, there was an input place there where they could flag, like, a customer.
It was in, like, the customer temperature and relationship pillar, where they, every week, had to go in and, like, switch things from red to yellow or green.
And that was, like, kind of the first systematization.
But where was that?
And, sorry, which tool?
Oh, okay.
Yeah, our very V1 customer health score was Google.
would, like, close a deal on HubSpot, then put the customer into Google Sheet?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Basically we were honestly pretty small.
think by the time I left, we had like 10 true customers.
So it wasn't, we weren't at a point where the scale was interfering with that process too much, but hoping to grow into a point where it might.
Yeah.
Oh, you froze for a second.
There, you're back.
Okay.
What, um, hold on.
Sorry.
I'm looking at my other.
Oh yeah.
Do you, are you typically the one like building dashboards and reporting and things like that?
I, I have been on both ends of that.
Um, I curated for you.
I was because I was really the only ops person.
So I think it wasn't going to get, is that someone with 10 customers?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We were a very small early stage startup.
Um, before that at Airtable, like much more mature company.
Yeah.
What, um, I would build some dashboards that were very customer.
Success Specific and build them in Airtable because we were working in Airtable.

But then we had a full-fledged data science team and so worked closely with them on scoping things out, outlining requirements, handing tickets over to them for more robust dashboards that really needed to plug into the backend utilization data that we were tracking.
Right, because it's like product usage stuff.
I feel like we don't really have that today.
We don't have a platform launched to customers yet.
So everything's services.
But yeah, our stuff is really spread out between like Notion is our kind of client database that people, the whole team uses.
And like, we're trying to put health scoring in there, but it's like not like it doesn't like track week over week changes and stuff.
Like it's not a database, a true database.
And then we have HubSpot, but again, like no one's in there except sales.
And then we do use Linear now, but it's more like when there's a health problem we like open.
The ticket.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Track that.
But like those three systems are not like connected.
And I don't know that they ever can be, or if there's like another tool out there or something.
Yeah.
And especially for like the trending tracking, like being able to see the change day over day.
I feel like I just like, do you remember how Airtable tracked like client health?
Yeah, we, yes.
So we had, we were looking often at utilization in like the last 28 day period so that we could get a sense of like over the last month, what, you know, how many people have logged in and used it?
What have they done?
We had this model of like, we called it a sophistication score internally, which might not be the nicest way to frame it, but like a sense of how savvy are people who's doing the building.
And when it came to the trending data, what we did was pull into, like, I pulled it into my personal air table, essentially a junction.
table that was like a customer and their health scores, like their health data on a specific date.

And so I could go both, I could go back in the spreadsheet and I could look at like, you know, November 13th, we have XYZ data, November 12th that looked like this, November 11th that looked like that.
But by, how did I do it?
I set up a dashboard where I could like see graphs trending over time.
Sorry, they had it in sheets as well?
Or you're saying like in an air table?
It was an air table, but air table, you can like use a little bit like a.
Yeah, like a script.
Okay.
Yeah.
So it was all an air table and it was pulled in.
It's like capturing like the data every day.
It was like daily snapshots and I forget exactly what I needed to do to connect it.
But once I connected like all of a client's inputs day over day, I was able to graph in the dashboard.
It's so crazy to me how like, I mean, we haven't looked at that many tools, but it just seems like something that's not really like out of the box at all.
dashboard Thank
Oh, yeah.
And it's so helpful.

Yeah, like if I could see like, you know, Lovable, our customer was like at this, this week, and then this, and this, like, and we closed this ticket to help, and then it went up here, and like, everything, like it just, that would be, I mean, you could make a beast spreadsheet, basically, where you have a good column per week or something like that.
Yeah.
But then, yeah, you don't want to have to maintain that.
It worked well, pulling it in automatically into Airtable, and I've had so much trouble in any other tool.
And there was coding involved, like there was a foundational part of it that someone else who was like more tech savvy than me had ramped up to start with.

Yeah, you know, one of the things that I thought was most helpful was just being able to see like the volatility, like I, I wanted to be able to see what the gap was, like the highest gaps in usage over the course of the quarter, for example, and I called those my big numbers, but just being able to see who's tanking in usage.
And who's spiking in usage and who spiked and then tanked.
just like seeing that range was so helpful.
Yeah.
What in the Zendesk example, like what was your personal like biggest accomplishment?
I think it was honestly just getting everyone over into it in a pretty frictionless way.
So it was less about the architecture of it and more about, you know, managing like leadership stakeholders and keeping them bought in week over week as we had our check-ins.

And then also doing the training for the merchandising team and then also training up a subset of the merchandising team to be like on a client team and also getting the customer success managers actually checking stuff in there.
You know, it was just like all of these different input points and all of this, probably like six or seven different specific kind of change management mentalities and like assets to deliver.
And just, you know, all things considered, there was like one day where one thing went wrong and everyone hopped on it so fast and fixed everything so quickly.
And I was like, oh, okay, this can run.
Like, they're good.
So it was really that like internal component of it, I think.
Any like mistake you made along the way?
Um, the one thing that went wrong is we had set it up such that our customers were really just using Slack if they used like a Slack integration with us to open tickets.
We didn't want them having to go in the platform.
And the reason for that was that down the road, we wanted to integrate it with our app.
So we wanted them to only have to log into curated for use application, and they would be able to see like open requests in the app.
And so there was like a downstream intention, but we didn't want to have too much like change management with our clients, have too much of that.
So the client would, okay, yeah, you don't want the client in Zendai.
You want the client in the platform seeing a ticket.
Okay.
Right, right.
And that just wasn't built yet.
So we were like input through Slack or email for a while.
And updates would get sent back out into the shared Slack channel with the client based off of the account that a ticket was tagged to in Zendesk.
And sometimes in like very specific circumstances, the account wouldn't auto tag correctly.
And I was keeping an eye on like, I had my own little dashboard of like common, like the account that they commonly were misattributed to so that I could go in and clean it and fix it.
And I was out of office one day.
And what ended up happening was an update sent to the wrong client channel.
So essentially like sent client information to the wrong client, which is awful.
But everyone hopped on it really quickly.
Someone delete, delete, delete.
Yeah, like the admin for Slack hopped in so fast.
I deleted that from the channel.
And, you know, then I did some digging and it was a training issue.

Like the person who owned that ticket who, you know, we'd been training up how do you talk to clients, how do you manage these tickets, how do you show the update the status of a ticket, like all of these different workflows.
And I had taken it for granted that we would make sure it was tied to the right account.
Like that seemed, as someone who's worked in tickets before, as someone who's like been customer facing before.
Or if I said it, like I feel like it would be an obvious thing to miss.
But if I said it, was probably very quickly in passing, check the account, check the contact, check if it's internal or not, and like moved on.
And she just didn't check.
So it was just tied to the wrong account.
So it was a very quick fix and it was a very quick like training opportunity and learning opportunity and it happened again.
But it was just that moment of like, oh, I probably to some extent took that for granted and maybe should have like hammered that home a little bit more given the audience and given the platform.
Yeah.
What would.
For the whole, like, Zendesk rollout, like, what would your manager have rated you a scale of 1 to 10?
10 being, like, that was the best ever.
Flawless.
I think highly.
I think probably the customer, I sort of, like, half rolled into customer success and half rolled into the COO, and I think the CSM probably would have said, like, 9, maybe.
And the COO, I mean, she spoke very highly of it.
think, though, that she was also fielding a lot of, like, imagination from other leadership stakeholders.
A lot of, like, oh, can Zendesk do this?
Can Zendesk do that?
And that could have maybe been tidier.
So maybe for her, like, an 8 or 9 as well, just in, like, the managing expectations and managing, like, sort of rampant brainstorming that happened through the whole process.
So the, and that, like, yeah, like, what would be, just so I make sure I hear it, like, what if.
What would be the difference between like eight or nine and then 10?
I think for the head of CS, I don't know, maybe I'm being critical of myself, but I'm putting a nine from her because it took her a very long time to actually hop into Zendesk to see anything.
Like she knew it was there and she knew it was working and she was very, she spoke very highly of like the improvements that it made in her day-to-day life.
And also months later would be slacking me like, hey, where do I go find tickets on my accounts?
And so I would assume that if she was like putting her own enablement into that score, that it would be maybe like a nine.
That's how I come with just not leading my example.
I know, I know.
And there's like only so many times you want to like nag upwards too and be like, hey, manager, can you log in, right?
Help me, help you.
Yeah, probably the same was true of the COO.
I don't think she needed to go in there.
She didn't need to see a lot of tech, she was operating at a level a little above that, but she just had no idea how to find dashboards for a while.
So, yeah, I think it was maybe stronger enablement and stronger need definition for these higher levels within the company that would sort of motivate that enablement.
How many people work in the company?
Good question.
The merchandising team was largest and they were maybe 40.
I don't know, 60, 65 people.
Okay.
Okay, cool.
Okay, I really do have a hard stop meeting with our founders.
So I shouldn't be late, but any like last minute things I can answer for you?
Otherwise, like usually I talk to Andy and our recruiter.
I mean, back to backs all day, but like by the end of the I hope we get back to you like by the end of the week, essentially tomorrow.
Okay.
Yeah, I guess I had a couple questions and we're right up at time.
I guess.
I
What would be the high priority things for you for this year?
When you think about where this role is hopping in and supporting from your viewpoint, what are the big items?
I think a lot of it's just like using this, like we have ideas there for like how to do some systems.
know they're not perfect at all, but like then there's just this, we sort of try to get everyone to do it.
And then there's just like completely no accountability on it at all.
We get to the meeting where we would review metrics or something and it's like nobody did any of the .
So it's like somehow can we like build in systems around that?
Like not that I want you to babysit them.
It's like how do we make it easier for them to use and like put some accountability into the systems themselves?
Yeah.
I think those are the main things.
Like so it's like taking like Andy and I have a lot of ideas for how it could work, but then it's hard for us to find time to like implement it, make it better.
Like we stick with V1 for too long because we're like, it was good enough.
Fair.
Okay.
Like taking things the next.
Like we're like almost going to be 15 million revenue at the end of the year.
Like we've like 70 clients, like a hundred people.
It's like, we need to go to next level.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Cool.
Well, it's nice to meet you.
No, no worries at all.
Good luck with your back-to-backs.
Thank you.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interviewing Emily Gutzmer for the Client Operations Manager role.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=333.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Automation Philosophy:** Automate low-value admin tasks to free up human capacity for strategic work. Implement changes incrementally to manage team adoption and avoid overwhelming them.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
  - [**Zendesk Rollout Success:** Successfully managed the Zendesk rollout for 40–65 people at Curated for You, achieving high adoption by using message snippets to train non-client-facing staff.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1390.0)
  - [**Critical Zendesk Mistake:** A training gap on account tagging caused client data to be sent to the wrong Slack channel. The team's rapid response contained the breach, highlighting the need for explicit, repeated training on critical workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1461.0)
  - [**Current State:** The company's ops are fragmented across Notion, HubSpot, and Linear, lacking a unified source of truth and accountability. The goal is to build systems that enforce processes and enable scaling.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1162.0)

## Topics

### Current Operational Challenges

  - [**Problem:** The company's ops are fragmented across Notion, HubSpot, and Linear, preventing a unified source of truth and accountability.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1162.0)
  - [**Pain Point:** V1 systems persist too long because leadership lacks time for implementation and improvement.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1843.0)
  - [**Goal:** Build systems that enforce processes and create accountability, moving beyond manual oversight to enable scaling.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1800.0)
  - [**Context:** The company is growing rapidly (\~$15M revenue, 70 clients, 100 people) and needs to mature its operations.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1843.0)

### Automation Philosophy & Examples

  - [**Core Principle:** Automate low-value admin tasks to free up human capacity for strategic work.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
  - [**Example:** Auto-create a project in a PM tool when a deal closes in the CRM.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=410.0)
  - [**Implementation Strategy:**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=494.0)
      - [Let teams feel manual pain first → validates the process before automating.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=494.0)
      - [Implement changes incrementally → manages team adoption and reduces overwhelm.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=528.0)
  - [**Small-Scale Example (Curated for You):**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=560.0)
      - [**Problem:** Non-client-facing staff (merchandising team) lacked diplomatic communication skills for Zendesk tickets.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=583.0)
      - [**Solution:** Created message snippets → provided a script for staff to use, building comfort and skill with client-facing language.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=583.0)

### Zendesk Rollout Case Study (Curated for You)

  - [**Project Goal:** Roll out Zendesk to 40–65 people and integrate it with Slack for client ticket submission.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1390.0)
  - [**Primary Success:** Smooth change management across multiple stakeholder groups (leadership, CSMs, merchandising team).](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1390.0)
  - [**Critical Mistake & Learning:**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1456.0)
      - [**Incident:** Client data was sent to the wrong Slack channel due to an incorrect account tag.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1461.0)
      - [**Root Cause:** A training gap. Emily assumed the "check account tag" step was obvious and didn't emphasize it enough for new users.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1561.0)
      - [**Resolution:** The team contained the breach immediately. The incident served as a key learning opportunity to make training more explicit and redundant.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1538.0)
  - [**Self-Assessment (Scale of 1–10):**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1618.0)
      - [**CS Head:** 9/10 (High impact, but manager was slow to adopt the tool).](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1618.0)
      - [**COO:** 8–9/10 (High impact, but could have managed leadership's feature requests more tightly).](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1618.0)

### Tooling & Reporting Experience

  - [**Tooling Stack (Curated for You):** HubSpot, Notion, Linear, and Zendesk.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=976.0)
  - [**Reporting Experience:**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1110.0)
      - [**Curated for You:** Built all dashboards as the sole ops person.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1110.0)
      - [**Airtable:** Scoped requirements for a data science team; built CS-specific dashboards in Airtable.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1130.0)
  - [**Airtable Health Score Dashboard:**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1218.0)
      - [**Method:** Used a junction table to create daily snapshots of customer health data.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1251.0)
      - [**Value:** Enabled trending analysis to identify volatility (spikes, drops) in metrics like usage, which Emily called "big numbers."](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=1361.0)
  - [**Current Tooling Experiments:**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=798.0)
      - [**Motion:** AI-powered task scheduler.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=798.0)
      - [**Obsidian:** Documentation and note-taking tool.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=896.0)
  - [**Zapier Experience:**](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=906.0)
      - [**Use Case:** Connected HubSpot to Notion.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=906.0)
      - [**Challenge:** Inconsistent data entry in HubSpot required manual workarounds.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=935.0)
      - [**Limitation:** The free plan's scale limit prevented full adoption.](https://fathom.video/share/tfvzEg5sdMQe7gBHBLCzh31rF3Ahrn4C?tab=summary&timestamp=906.0)
